title: Deliberate Self-Harm
oneLiner: Intentional self-poisoning or self-injury; must immediately rule out airway obstruction, haemorrhage, and cardiotoxic ingestion.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction (hanging/strangulation):** stridor, neck ecchymosis, or hoarseness — this is *not* a behavioural patient; this is a surgical airway emergency.
- **Haemorrhagic shock (deep lacerations/arterial injury):** systolic BP <90 mmHg with uncontrolled bleeding — do not waste time on details; direct pressure, tourniquet, call vascular/ED senior.
- **Cardiotoxic overdose (TCA, beta-blocker, CCB):** widened QRS >100ms OR hypotension with bradycardia — antidote (bicarb) or pacing may be needed within minutes.
sections.Presentation:
- **History:** precise details of ingestion (drug, dose, time), method of injury (cutting, hanging, gassing), last meal, alcohol use, previous self-harm, suicidal intent.
- **Symptoms:** altered consciousness, difficulty breathing (if hanging), bleeding, pain at site, nausea/vomiting (overdose).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS, neck marks/ligature marks, respiratory effort, heart rate/rhythm, BP, skin (cyanosis, pallor, sweating).
  - Wounds: depth, location, arterial or venous bleeding, signs of infection.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Deliberate self-harm** (intentional) vs. **accidental poisoning** (no intent, e.g., child, medication error) — history of intent and context.
- **Factitious disorder / malingering** (consciously fabricated but with secondary gain) — patient may be evasive, inconsistencies in history, no genuine suicidal intent.
- **Medical mimic of self-harm** (e.g., accidental fall causing wound, hypoglycaemia from insulin accident) — objective evidence of alternative mechanism.
- **Splitter:** clear, consistent history of intent and planning (e.g., suicide note, told family) → **DSH**; no intent or secretive behaviour with external gain → **factitious/malingering**.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG (especially for QRS width, QTc, arrhythmia) — before any treatment.  
  - BM (hypoglycaemia common in insulin/oral hypoglycaemic OD).  
  - Urine pregnancy test (women of childbearing age).  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, coagulation screen, lactate.  
  - Paracetamol & salicylate levels (even if history negative — co-ingestion).  
  - Toxicology screen if unknown drug (send to lab; results may take hours).  
  - Serial levels for paracetamol, salicylate, theophylline, lithium, etc.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR if suspected aspiration, pneumothorax (stabbing), or pulmonary oedema (opioid overdose).  
  - CT head if altered GCS <15 with no clear toxic cause.  
  - AXR if body packer or iron/sustained-release ingestion (not routine).  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Paracetamol nomogram (use treatment line: start NAC if level above line at 4h+).  
  - ToxBase (UK) – online resource for antidotes and management.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum paracetamol level** – any overdose, any time, even if history says other drug. Delay leads to preventable liver failure.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **A–E:** Airway (jaw thrust, chin lift, consider intubation if GCS <8 or stridor), Breathing (oxygen, treat hypoxia), Circulation (IV access, fluids, control bleeding).  
  - **Decontamination:** activated charcoal 50g PO (if within 1h of ingestion, airway protected); NOT if reduced GCS/aspiration risk.  
  - **Antidotes:** NAC for paracetamol (see Drugs); naloxone 0.4–2mg IV for opioid overdose; flumazenil only by senior (risk of seizures).  
  - **ECG monitoring** for cardiotoxic drugs.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Medical management of specific ingestions (e.g., paracetamol: observe for hepatotoxicity; monitor LFTs/INR at 24h, 48h; recheck paracetamol level after NAC if late presentation).  
  - Wound care: clean, suture, tetanus prophylaxis if needed, neurovascular check.  
  - 1:1 observation for acute suicide risk (local policy).  
  - Liaise with psychiatry (urgent mental health assessment within 24h or sooner if high risk).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Referral to community mental health team (CMHT) or crisis resolution home treatment team.  
  - Safety planning, psychological therapy (CBT, DBT).  
  - Medication review (mood stabilisers, antidepressants – do not switch abruptly).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1L over 8h if nil oral; otherwise oral fluids encouraged.  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** yes (unless anticoagulated or coagulopathic e.g., liver failure).  
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for surgical/toxicological reasons (e.g., suspected surgical bleeding).  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 – hourly for first 4h then 4–6 hourly if stable; cardiac monitoring if on cardiotoxic drugs until QRS normalised.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **N-acetylcysteine (NAC) for paracetamol overdose:**  
    - **IV three-bag regimen (if weight >40kg, normal liver function):**  
      - Bag 1: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL D5W over 1 hour.  
      - Bag 2: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL D5W over 4 hours.  
      - Bag 3: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL D5W over 16 hours.  
    - **If weight <40kg or hepatic impairment:** [check local policy – lower volumes].  
  - **Naloxone for opioid overdose:** 0.4–2 mg IV, repeat every 2–3 min if no response; up to 10 mg total.  
    - May need infusion (e.g., 0.4 mg/h) for long-acting opioids.  
  - **Activated charcoal:** 50 g PO single dose (if within 1 h of ingestion, airway protected).  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Oral NAC** (not used in acute UK practice unless IV access impossible).  
  - **Flumazenil** (for benzodiazepine overdose) – only by anaesthesia/ITU due to seizure risk.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NAC: anaphylactoid reactions (rash, bronchospasm) – stop infusion, give antihistamine, restart at slower rate if mild.  
  - Naloxone: precipitates acute withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, hypertension) – use smallest effective dose.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NAC does not need dose adjustment in renal impairment; dose in hepatic encephalopathy? Check local policy.  
  - Naloxone: no dose adjustment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **NAC infusion rate error** – too fast causes histamine release and hypotension; too slow delays protection. Use a standardised protocol chart.  
  - **Giving flumazenil in mixed overdose** (e.g., tricyclic + benzodiazepine) – may provoke seizures and cardiac arrest.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Hepatic failure (paracetamol), arrhythmias (TCA, digoxin), hypoglycaemia (insulin/oral hypoglycaemics), rhabdomyolysis (prolonged immobility, compression), aspiration pneumonia, wound infection/necrosis, compartment syndrome (from limb injury).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Repeated self-harm, suicidal behaviour, chronic pain syndromes, scarring, neuropathy (from toxicity or trauma).
sections.Prognosis:
- Medical prognosis excellent if treated early (e.g., paracetamol – <8h from ingestion). Late presentation or multi-drug overdose worsens outcome.  
- Psychosocial prognosis highly variable – up to 10% die by suicide within 10 years of DSH; a single act does not accurately predict next attempt.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a paracetamol level in any overdose** – even if the patient says 'only alcohol' – because co-ingestion is common and silent.  
- **Perform ECG on every overdose** – a normal QRS makes TCA toxicity very unlikely.  
- **Ensure 1:1 observation** for any patient deemed high risk of further self-harm or suicide while on the ward – check local policy for level of observation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Discharging a DSH patient without psychiatric assessment** – even if medically cleared. This is a serious error; wait for mental health team review.  
- **Assuming an empty stomach from history** – never delay NAC if paracetamol level is above treatment line (even if prolonged).  
- **Giving flumazenil empirically** – only use under senior guidance in pure benzo overdose (and prepare for seizures).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  1. Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or yellowing of skin/eyes (hepatotoxicity – within 72h of overdose).  
  2. New or worsening drowsiness, confusion, or breathing difficulty (delayed toxicity).  
  3. Strong urges to self-harm again or suicidal thoughts.  
- **Follow-up:** crisis team or CMHT within 24–48 hours. Provide crisis line number (e.g., Samaritans 116 123). GP notified.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - GCS <13 or deteriorating.  
  - NEWS2 ≥7.  
  - New arrhythmia (wide QRS, ventricular tachycardia, sinus bradycardia with hypotension).  
  - Seizures.  
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.2, lactate >4).  
  - Suspected liver failure (INR rising, jaundice, encephalopathy).  
- **Call crash team / ITU if:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, airway compromise, intubation needed.  
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids (systolic BP <80 mmHg).  
  - Concern for airway loss (e.g., progressive neck swelling after hanging).

rawMarkdown:
## Deliberate Self-Harm

**One-liner:** Intentional self-poisoning or self-injury; must immediately rule out airway obstruction, haemorrhage, and cardiotoxic ingestion.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction (hanging/strangulation):** stridor, neck ecchymosis, or hoarseness — this is *not* a behavioural patient; this is a surgical airway emergency.
- **Haemorrhagic shock (deep lacerations/arterial injury):** systolic BP <90 mmHg with uncontrolled bleeding — do not waste time on details; direct pressure, tourniquet, call vascular/ED senior.
- **Cardiotoxic overdose (TCA, beta-blocker, CCB):** widened QRS >100ms OR hypotension with bradycardia — antidote (bicarb) or pacing may be needed within minutes.

### Presentation
- **History:** precise details of ingestion (drug, dose, time), method of injury (cutting, hanging, gassing), last meal, alcohol use, previous self-harm, suicidal intent.
- **Symptoms:** altered consciousness, difficulty breathing (if hanging), bleeding, pain at site, nausea/vomiting (overdose).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS, neck marks/ligature marks, respiratory effort, heart rate/rhythm, BP, skin (cyanosis, pallor, sweating).
  - Wounds: depth, location, arterial or venous bleeding, signs of infection.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Deliberate self-harm** (intentional) vs. **accidental poisoning** (no intent, e.g., child, medication error) — history of intent and context.
- **Factitious disorder / malingering** (consciously fabricated but with secondary gain) — patient may be evasive, inconsistencies in history, no genuine suicidal intent.
- **Medical mimic of self-harm** (e.g., accidental fall causing wound, hypoglycaemia from insulin accident) — objective evidence of alternative mechanism.
- **Splitter:** clear, consistent history of intent and planning (e.g., suicide note, told family) → **DSH**; no intent or secretive behaviour with external gain → **factitious/malingering**.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG (especially for QRS width, QTc, arrhythmia) — before any treatment.  
  - BM (hypoglycaemia common in insulin/oral hypoglycaemic OD).  
  - Urine pregnancy test (women of childbearing age).  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, coagulation screen, lactate.  
  - Paracetamol & salicylate levels (even if history negative — co-ingestion).  
  - Toxicology screen if unknown drug (send to lab; results may take hours).  
  - Serial levels for paracetamol, salicylate, theophylline, lithium, etc.  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR if suspected aspiration, pneumothorax (stabbing), or pulmonary oedema (opioid overdose).  
  - CT head if altered GCS <15 with no clear toxic cause.  
  - AXR if body packer or iron/sustained-release ingestion (not routine).  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Paracetamol nomogram (use treatment line: start NAC if level above line at 4h+).  
  - ToxBase (UK) – online resource for antidotes and management.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Serum paracetamol level** – any overdose, any time, even if history says other drug. Delay leads to preventable liver failure.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **A–E:** Airway (jaw thrust, chin lift, consider intubation if GCS <8 or stridor), Breathing (oxygen, treat hypoxia), Circulation (IV access, fluids, control bleeding).  
  - **Decontamination:** activated charcoal 50g PO (if within 1h of ingestion, airway protected); NOT if reduced GCS/aspiration risk.  
  - **Antidotes:** NAC for paracetamol (see Drugs); naloxone 0.4–2mg IV for opioid overdose; flumazenil only by senior (risk of seizures).  
  - **ECG monitoring** for cardiotoxic drugs.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Medical management of specific ingestions (e.g., paracetamol: observe for hepatotoxicity; monitor LFTs/INR at 24h, 48h; recheck paracetamol level after NAC if late presentation).  
  - Wound care: clean, suture, tetanus prophylaxis if needed, neurovascular check.  
  - 1:1 observation for acute suicide risk (local policy).  
  - Liaise with psychiatry (urgent mental health assessment within 24h or sooner if high risk).  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Referral to community mental health team (CMHT) or crisis resolution home treatment team.  
  - Safety planning, psychological therapy (CBT, DBT).  
  - Medication review (mood stabilisers, antidepressants – do not switch abruptly).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% saline 1L over 8h if nil oral; otherwise oral fluids encouraged.  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** yes (unless anticoagulated or coagulopathic e.g., liver failure).  
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for surgical/toxicological reasons (e.g., suspected surgical bleeding).  
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 – hourly for first 4h then 4–6 hourly if stable; cardiac monitoring if on cardiotoxic drugs until QRS normalised.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **N-acetylcysteine (NAC) for paracetamol overdose:**  
    - **IV three-bag regimen (if weight >40kg, normal liver function):**  
      - Bag 1: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL D5W over 1 hour.  
      - Bag 2: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL D5W over 4 hours.  
      - Bag 3: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL D5W over 16 hours.  
    - **If weight <40kg or hepatic impairment:** [check local policy – lower volumes].  
  - **Naloxone for opioid overdose:** 0.4–2 mg IV, repeat every 2–3 min if no response; up to 10 mg total.  
    - May need infusion (e.g., 0.4 mg/h) for long-acting opioids.  
  - **Activated charcoal:** 50 g PO single dose (if within 1 h of ingestion, airway protected).  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - **Oral NAC** (not used in acute UK practice unless IV access impossible).  
  - **Flumazenil** (for benzodiazepine overdose) – only by anaesthesia/ITU due to seizure risk.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NAC: anaphylactoid reactions (rash, bronchospasm) – stop infusion, give antihistamine, restart at slower rate if mild.  
  - Naloxone: precipitates acute withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, hypertension) – use smallest effective dose.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NAC does not need dose adjustment in renal impairment; dose in hepatic encephalopathy? Check local policy.  
  - Naloxone: no dose adjustment.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **NAC infusion rate error** – too fast causes histamine release and hypotension; too slow delays protection. Use a standardised protocol chart.  
  - **Giving flumazenil in mixed overdose** (e.g., tricyclic + benzodiazepine) – may provoke seizures and cardiac arrest.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Hepatic failure (paracetamol), arrhythmias (TCA, digoxin), hypoglycaemia (insulin/oral hypoglycaemics), rhabdomyolysis (prolonged immobility, compression), aspiration pneumonia, wound infection/necrosis, compartment syndrome (from limb injury).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Repeated self-harm, suicidal behaviour, chronic pain syndromes, scarring, neuropathy (from toxicity or trauma).

### Prognosis
- Medical prognosis excellent if treated early (e.g., paracetamol – <8h from ingestion). Late presentation or multi-drug overdose worsens outcome.  
- Psychosocial prognosis highly variable – up to 10% die by suicide within 10 years of DSH; a single act does not accurately predict next attempt.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check a paracetamol level in any overdose** – even if the patient says 'only alcohol' – because co-ingestion is common and silent.  
- **Perform ECG on every overdose** – a normal QRS makes TCA toxicity very unlikely.  
- **Ensure 1:1 observation** for any patient deemed high risk of further self-harm or suicide while on the ward – check local policy for level of observation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Discharging a DSH patient without psychiatric assessment** – even if medically cleared. This is a serious error; wait for mental health team review.  
- **Assuming an empty stomach from history** – never delay NAC if paracetamol level is above treatment line (even if prolonged).  
- **Giving flumazenil empirically** – only use under senior guidance in pure benzo overdose (and prepare for seizures).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  1. Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or yellowing of skin/eyes (hepatotoxicity – within 72h of overdose).  
  2. New or worsening drowsiness, confusion, or breathing difficulty (delayed toxicity).  
  3. Strong urges to self-harm again or suicidal thoughts.  
- **Follow-up:** crisis team or CMHT within 24–48 hours. Provide crisis line number (e.g., Samaritans 116 123). GP notified.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - GCS <13 or deteriorating.  
  - NEWS2 ≥7.  
  - New arrhythmia (wide QRS, ventricular tachycardia, sinus bradycardia with hypotension).  
  - Seizures.  
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.2, lactate >4).  
  - Suspected liver failure (INR rising, jaundice, encephalopathy).  
- **Call crash team / ITU if:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, airway compromise, intubation needed.  
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids (systolic BP <80 mmHg).  
  - Concern for airway loss (e.g., progressive neck swelling after hanging).
